The **United Nations** (**UN**) is a global [intergovernmental organization](/wiki/Intergovernmental_organization "Intergovernmental organization") established by the signing of the [UN Charter](/wiki/Charter_of_the_United_Nations "Charter of the United Nations") on 26 June 1945 with the articulated mission of maintaining [international peace](/wiki/International_peace "International peace") and [security](/wiki/International_security "International security"), to develop friendly [relations](/wiki/Diplomacy "Diplomacy") among [states](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)"), to promote international cooperation, and to serve as a centre for harmonizing the actions of states in achieving those goals.

The [United Nations headquarters](/wiki/United_Nations_headquarters "United Nations headquarters") is located in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), with several other offices located in [Geneva](/wiki/United_Nations_Office_at_Geneva "United Nations Office at Geneva"), [Nairobi](/wiki/United_Nations_Office_at_Nairobi "United Nations Office at Nairobi"), [Vienna](/wiki/United_Nations_Office_at_Vienna "United Nations Office at Vienna"), and [The Hague](/wiki/The_Hague "The Hague"). The UN comprises six principal organizations: the [General Assembly](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly "United Nations General Assembly"), the [Security Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council "United Nations Security Council"), the [Economic and Social Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Economic_and_Social_Council "United Nations Economic and Social Council"), the [International Court of Justice](/wiki/International_Court_of_Justice "International Court of Justice"), the [Secretariat](/wiki/United_Nations_Secretariat "United Nations Secretariat"), and the [Trusteeship Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Trusteeship_Council "United Nations Trusteeship Council") which, together with several [specialized agencies](/wiki/List_of_specialized_agencies_of_the_United_Nations "List of specialized agencies of the United Nations") and related agencies, make up the [United Nations System](/wiki/United_Nations_System "United Nations System"). There are in total 193 [member states](/wiki/Member_states_of_the_United_Nations "Member states of the United Nations"), representing nearly all of the world's [sovereign states](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state"), as well as two [observer states](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly_observers "United Nations General Assembly observers"), the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") and the [State of Palestine](/wiki/Palestine "Palestine").

The UN has primarily focused on economic and social development, particularly during the wave of [decolonization](/wiki/Decolonization "Decolonization") in the mid-20th century. The UN currently operates [11 peacekeeping missions](/wiki/United_Nations_peacekeeping "United Nations peacekeeping"), by order of inception, in [the Middle East](/wiki/United_Nations_Truce_Supervision_Organization "United Nations Truce Supervision Organization"), [Kashmir](/wiki/UN_mediation_of_the_Kashmir_dispute "UN mediation of the Kashmir dispute"), [Cyprus](/wiki/United_Nations_Peacekeeping_Force_in_Cyprus "United Nations Peacekeeping Force in Cyprus"), the [Golan Heights](/wiki/United_Nations_Disengagement_Observer_Force "United Nations Disengagement Observer Force"), [Lebanon](/wiki/United_Nations_Interim_Force_in_Lebanon "United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon"), the [Western Sahara](/wiki/United_Nations_Mission_for_the_Referendum_in_Western_Sahara "United Nations Mission for the Referendum in Western Sahara"), [Kosovo](/wiki/United_Nations_Interim_Administration_Mission_in_Kosovo "United Nations Interim Administration Mission in Kosovo"), [Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/MONUSCO "MONUSCO"), [Sudan](/wiki/United_Nations_Interim_Security_Force_for_Abyei "United Nations Interim Security Force for Abyei"), [South Sudan](/wiki/United_Nations_Mission_in_South_Sudan "United Nations Mission in South Sudan"), and the [Central African Republic](/wiki/MINUSCA "MINUSCA"). The UN has been recognized as a leader of peace and human development, with many officers and agencies having been awarded the [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize"), but has also been [criticized for perceived ineffectiveness, bias, and corruption](/wiki/Criticism_of_the_United_Nations "Criticism of the United Nations").

## History

Main article: [History of the United Nations](/wiki/History_of_the_United_Nations "History of the United Nations")

### Background (pre-1941)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Emperor_Haile_Selassie_League_of_Nations_speech.png/250px-Emperor_Haile_Selassie_League_of_Nations_speech.png)](/wiki/File:Emperor_Haile_Selassie_League_of_Nations_speech.png)

[Haile Selassie I](/wiki/Haile_Selassie "Haile Selassie") at the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") appealing Italy's 1936 invasion in which the League failed to intervene.

In the century prior to the UN's creation, several [international organizations](/wiki/International_organization "International organization") such as the [International Committee of the Red Cross](/wiki/International_Committee_of_the_Red_Cross "International Committee of the Red Cross") were formed to ensure protection and assistance for victims of armed conflict and strife.[[4]](#cite_note-5)

During [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), several major leaders, especially [U.S. president](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Foreign_policy_of_the_Woodrow_Wilson_administration "Foreign policy of the Woodrow Wilson administration"), advocated for a world body to guarantee peace. The winners of the war, the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I"), met to decide on formal peace terms at the [Paris Peace Conference](/wiki/Paris_Peace_Conference_(1919%E2%80%931920) "Paris Peace Conference (1919–1920)"). The [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") was approved and started operations, but the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") never joined. On 10 January 1920, the League of Nations formally came into being when the [Covenant of the League of Nations](/wiki/Covenant_of_the_League_of_Nations "Covenant of the League of Nations"), ratified by 42 nations in 1919, took effect.[[5]](#cite_note-6) The League Council acted as an executive body directing the Assembly's business. It began with four permanent members—the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), and [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan").

After some limited successes and failures during the 1920s, the League proved ineffective in the 1930s, as it failed to act against the [Japanese invasion of Manchuria](/wiki/Japanese_invasion_of_Manchuria "Japanese invasion of Manchuria") in 1933.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Forty nations voted for Japan to withdraw from [Manchuria](/wiki/Manchuria "Manchuria") but Japan voted against it and walked out of the League instead of withdrawing from Manchuria.[[6]](#cite_note-7) It also failed to act against the [Second Italo-Ethiopian War](/wiki/Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War "Second Italo-Ethiopian War"), after the appeal for international intervention by [Ethiopian Emperor](/wiki/Ethiopian_Emperor "Ethiopian Emperor") [Haile Selassie I](/wiki/Haile_Selassie "Haile Selassie") at Geneva in 1936 went with no avail, including when calls for economic sanctions against Italy failed. Italy and other nations left the League.[[7]](#cite_note-8)

When [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") broke out in 1939, the League effectively closed down.[[8]](#cite_note-9)

### Declarations by the Allies of World War II (1941–1944)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/United_Nations_organization_sketch_by_Franklin_Roosevelt_with_the_Four_Policemen_in_1943.jpg/250px-United_Nations_organization_sketch_by_Franklin_Roosevelt_with_the_Four_Policemen_in_1943.jpg)](/wiki/File:United_Nations_organization_sketch_by_Franklin_Roosevelt_with_the_Four_Policemen_in_1943.jpg)

1943 sketch by Franklin Roosevelt of the UN original three branches: The [Four Policemen](/wiki/Four_Policemen "Four Policemen"), an executive branch, and an international assembly of forty UN member states.

The first step towards the establishment of the United Nations was the Inter-Allied Conference in London that led to the [Declaration of St James's Palace](/wiki/Declaration_of_St_James%27s_Palace "Declaration of St James's Palace") on 12 June 1941.[[9]](#cite_note-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11) By August 1941, American president [Franklin Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") and British prime minister [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill") had drafted the [Atlantic Charter](/wiki/Atlantic_Charter "Atlantic Charter"); which defined goals for the post-war world. At the subsequent meeting of the Inter-Allied Council in London on 24 September 1941, the eight [governments in exile of countries under Axis occupation](/wiki/List_of_governments_in_exile_during_World_War_II "List of governments in exile during World War II"), together with the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") and representatives of the [Free French Forces](/wiki/Free_French_Forces "Free French Forces"), unanimously adopted adherence to the common principles of policy set forth by Britain and the United States.[[11]](#cite_note-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13)

Roosevelt and Churchill met at the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House") in December 1941 for the [Arcadia Conference](/wiki/Arcadia_Conference "Arcadia Conference"). Roosevelt is considered a founder of the UN,[[13]](#cite_note-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15) and coined the term *United Nations* to describe the [Allied countries](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II").[[15]](#cite_note-16) Churchill accepted it, noting its use by [Lord Byron](/wiki/Lord_Byron "Lord Byron").[[16]](#cite_note-17) The text of the [Declaration by United Nations](/wiki/Declaration_by_United_Nations "Declaration by United Nations") was drafted on 29 December 1941, by Roosevelt, Churchill, and [Harry Hopkins](/wiki/Harry_Hopkins "Harry Hopkins"). It incorporated Soviet suggestions but included no role for France. One major change from the Atlantic Charter was the addition of a provision for [religious freedom](/wiki/Religious_freedom "Religious freedom"), which Stalin approved after Roosevelt insisted.[[17]](#cite_note-18)[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherwood1948447–453-19)

Roosevelt's idea of the "[Four Powers](/wiki/Four_Policemen "Four Policemen")", refers to the four major Allied countries, the United States, the United Kingdom, the Soviet Union, and [China](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912%E2%80%931949) "Republic of China (1912–1949)"), emerged in the Declaration by the United Nations.[[19]](#cite_note-sheriff-20) On New Year's Day 1942, Roosevelt, Churchill, the Soviet Union's former Foreign Minister [Maxim Litvinov](/wiki/Maxim_Litvinov "Maxim Litvinov"), and the Chinese Premier [T. V. Soong](/wiki/T._V._Soong "T. V. Soong") signed the "[Declaration by United Nations](/wiki/Declaration_by_United_Nations "Declaration by United Nations")",[[20]](#cite_note-21) and the next day the representatives of twenty-two other nations added their signatures. During the war, the United Nations became the official term for the Allies. In order to join, countries had to sign the Declaration and declare war on the [Axis powers](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers").[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchlesinger2003-22)

The October 1943 [Moscow Conference](/wiki/Moscow_Conference_(1943) "Moscow Conference (1943)") resulted in the [Moscow Declarations](/wiki/Moscow_Declarations "Moscow Declarations"), including the [Four Power Declaration on General Security](/wiki/Declaration_of_the_Four_Nations "Declaration of the Four Nations"). This declaration was signed by the [Allied Big Four](/wiki/Big_Four_(World_War_II) "Big Four (World War II)")—the United States, the Soviet Union, the United Kingdom, and China—and aimed for the creation "at the earliest possible date of a general international organization". This was the first public announcement that a new international organization was being contemplated to replace the League of Nations. The [Tehran Conference](/wiki/Tehran_Conference "Tehran Conference") followed shortly afterwards at which Roosevelt, Churchill and [Joseph Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin"), the leader of the Soviet Union, met and discussed the idea of a post-war international organization.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The new international organization was formulated and negotiated amongst the delegations from the [Allied Big Four](/wiki/Big_Four_(World_War_II) "Big Four (World War II)") at the [Dumbarton Oaks Conference](/wiki/Dumbarton_Oaks_Conference "Dumbarton Oaks Conference") from 21 September to 7 October 1944. They agreed on proposals for the aims, structure and functioning of the new organization.[[22]](#cite_note-Bohlen_1973-23)[[23]](#cite_note-24)[[24]](#cite_note-25) It took the [conference at Yalta](/wiki/Yalta_Conference "Yalta Conference") in February 1945, and further negotiations with the Soviet Union, before all the issues were resolved.[[22]](#cite_note-Bohlen_1973-23)

### Founding (1945)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/United_Nations_Member_States-1945.png/500px-United_Nations_Member_States-1945.png)](/wiki/File:United_Nations_Member_States-1945.png)

The UN in 1945: founding members in light blue, protectorates and territories of the founding members in dark blue (Some nations' land would be different from now.)

By 1 March 1945, 21 additional states had signed the Declaration by the United Nations.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOsmańczyk20042445-26) After months of planning, the [UN Conference on International Organization](/wiki/United_Nations_Conference_on_International_Organization "United Nations Conference on International Organization") opened in [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco") on 25 April 1945. It was attended by 50 nations' governments and a number of non-governmental organizations.[[26]](#cite_note-Charter-27)[[27]](#cite_note-UNHistory-28)[[28]](#cite_note-29) The delegations of the Big Four chaired the plenary meetings.[[29]](#cite_note-30) Previously, Churchill had urged Roosevelt to restore France to its status of a major power after the [liberation of Paris](/wiki/Liberation_of_Paris "Liberation of Paris") in August 1944. The drafting of the [Charter of the United Nations](/wiki/Charter_of_the_United_Nations "Charter of the United Nations") was completed over the following two months, and it was signed on 26 June 1945 by the representatives of the 50 countries.[[30]](#cite_note-31)[[31]](#cite_note-32) The UN officially came into existence at 20:07 ([UTC](/wiki/Coordinated_Universal_Time "Coordinated Universal Time")) on 24 October 1945,[[32]](#cite_note-33)[[33]](#cite_note-34) upon ratification of the Charter by the five permanent members of the [Security Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council "United Nations Security Council"): the United States, the United Kingdom, France, the Soviet Union and [China](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912%E2%80%931949) "Republic of China (1912–1949)") — and by a majority of the other 46 nations.[[34]](#cite_note-unmilestones1941to1950-35)[[35]](#cite_note-original_draft-36)

The [first meetings](/wiki/First_session_of_the_United_Nations_General_Assembly "First session of the United Nations General Assembly") of the [General Assembly](/wiki/General_Assembly "General Assembly"), with 51 nations represented,[[b]](#cite_note-39) and the Security Council took place in [London](/wiki/London "London") beginning in January 1946.[[34]](#cite_note-unmilestones1941to1950-35) Debates began at once, covering topical issues such as the presence of Russian troops in [Iranian Azerbaijan](/wiki/Iranian_Azerbaijan "Iranian Azerbaijan") and British forces in [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece").[[38]](#cite_note-40) British diplomat [Gladwyn Jebb](/wiki/Gladwyn_Jebb "Gladwyn Jebb") served as interim secretary-general.

The General Assembly selected [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") as the site for the headquarters of the UN. Construction began on 14 September 1948 and the facility was completed on 9 October 1952. The Norwegian Foreign Minister, [Trygve Lie](/wiki/Trygve_Lie "Trygve Lie"), was the first elected [UN secretary-general](/wiki/Secretary-General_of_the_United_Nations "Secretary-General of the United Nations").[[34]](#cite_note-unmilestones1941to1950-35)

### Cold War (1947–1991)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Dag_Hammarskj%C3%B6ld_%283x4_cropped%29.jpg/250px-Dag_Hammarskj%C3%B6ld_%283x4_cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dag_Hammarskj%C3%B6ld_(3x4_cropped).jpg)

[Dag Hammarskjöld](/wiki/Dag_Hammarskj%C3%B6ld "Dag Hammarskjöld") was a particularly active secretary-general from 1953 until he died in 1961.

Though the UN's primary mandate was [peacekeeping](/wiki/Peacekeeping "Peacekeeping"), the division between the United States and the Soviet Union often paralysed the organization; generally allowing it to intervene only in conflicts distant from the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War").[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeisler199535-41) Two notable exceptions were a Security Council resolution on 7 July 1950 authorizing a [US-led coalition](/wiki/United_Nations_Command "United Nations Command") to repel the [North Korean invasion of South Korea](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War"), passed in the absence of the Soviet Union,[[34]](#cite_note-unmilestones1941to1950-35)[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeisler199558–59-42) and the signing of the [Korean Armistice Agreement](/wiki/Korean_Armistice_Agreement "Korean Armistice Agreement") on 27 July 1953.[[41]](#cite_note-43)

On 29 November 1947, the General Assembly approved [Resolution 181](/wiki/United_Nations_Partition_Plan_for_Palestine "United Nations Partition Plan for Palestine"), a proposal to partition British Mandatory [Palestine](/wiki/Mandatory_Palestine "Mandatory Palestine") into two states, a Jewish state and an Arab state, with Jerusalem placed under international status.[[42]](#cite_note-44) The Partition plan passed 33–13 with 10 abstentions and one absent. The plan was accepted by the Jews and rejected by the Arabs of Palestine and the Arab states [[43]](#cite_note-186-20240719-45) leading to [civil war](/wiki/1947%E2%80%931948_civil_war_in_Mandatory_Palestine "1947–1948 civil war in Mandatory Palestine"). Following the declaration of the State of Israel on 15 May 1948, the surrounding Arab armies [invaded](https://archive.nytimes.com/www.nytimes.com/learning/aol/onthisday/big/0514.html) Palestine, beginning the [1948 Arab–Israeli War](/wiki/1948_Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_War "1948 Arab–Israeli War").[[44]](#cite_note-46) Two years later, [Ralph Bunche](/wiki/Ralph_Bunche "Ralph Bunche"), a UN official, negotiated [an armistice](/wiki/1949_Armistice_Agreements "1949 Armistice Agreements") to the [resulting conflict](/wiki/1948_Palestine_war "1948 Palestine war"), with the Security Council deciding that "an armistice shall be established in all sectors of Palestine".[[43]](#cite_note-186-20240719-45)[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeisler199551–54-47) On 7 November 1956, the [first UN peacekeeping force](/wiki/United_Nations_Emergency_Force "United Nations Emergency Force") was established to end the [Suez Crisis](/wiki/Suez_Crisis "Suez Crisis");[[46]](#cite_note-unmilestones1951to1960-48) however, the UN was unable to intervene against the Soviet Union's simultaneous invasion of [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary"), following [the country's revolution](/wiki/Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956 "Hungarian Revolution of 1956").[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeisler1995114-49)

On 14 July 1960, the UN established the [United Nations Operation in the Congo](/wiki/United_Nations_Operation_in_the_Congo "United Nations Operation in the Congo") (or UNOC), the largest military force of its early decades, to bring order to [Katanga](/wiki/State_of_Katanga "State of Katanga"), restoring it to the control of the [Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Republic_of_the_Congo_(L%C3%A9opoldville) "Republic of the Congo (Léopoldville)") by 11 May 1964.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeisler1995115–134-50) While travelling to meet rebel leader [Moise Tshombe](/wiki/Moise_Tshombe "Moise Tshombe") during the conflict, [Dag Hammarskjöld](/wiki/Dag_Hammarskj%C3%B6ld "Dag Hammarskjöld"), often named as one of the UN's most effective secretaries-general,[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeisler199576-51)[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy200760-52)[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFasulo200417,_20-53) [died in a plane crash](/wiki/1961_Ndola_United_Nations_DC-6_crash "1961 Ndola United Nations DC-6 crash"). Months later he was posthumously awarded the [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize").[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeisler1995127–128,_134-54) In 1964, Hammarskjöld's successor, [U Thant](/wiki/U_Thant "U Thant"), deployed the [UN Peacekeeping Force in Cyprus](/wiki/United_Nations_Peacekeeping_Force_in_Cyprus "United Nations Peacekeeping Force in Cyprus"), which would become one of the UN's longest-running peacekeeping missions.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeisler1995156–157-55)

With the spread of [decolonization](/wiki/Decolonization "Decolonization") in the 1960s, the UN's membership shot up due to an influx of newly independent nations. In 1960 alone, 17 new states joined the UN, 16 of them from Africa.[[46]](#cite_note-unmilestones1951to1960-48) On 25 October 1971, with opposition from the United States, but with the support of many [Third World](/wiki/Third_World "Third World") nations, the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/China "China") was given the Chinese seat on the Security Council in place of the [Republic of China](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") (also known as Taiwan). The vote was widely seen as a sign of waning American influence in the organization.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeisler1995195–197-56) Third World nations organized themselves into the [Group of 77](/wiki/Group_of_77 "Group of 77") under the leadership of Algeria, which briefly became a dominant power at the UN.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeisler1995208–210-57) On 10 November 1975, a bloc comprising the Soviet Union and Third World nations passed [a resolution](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly_Resolution_3379 "United Nations General Assembly Resolution 3379"), over strenuous American and Israeli opposition, declaring [Zionism](/wiki/Zionism "Zionism") to be a form of [racism](/wiki/Racism "Racism"). The resolution was repealed on 16 December 1991, shortly after the end of the Cold War.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeisler1995204–226,_213,_220–221-58)[[57]](#cite_note-59)

With an increasing Third World presence and the failure of UN mediation in conflicts in the [Middle East](/wiki/Six-Day_War "Six-Day War"), [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"), and [Kashmir](/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_War_of_1965 "Indo-Pakistani War of 1965"), the UN increasingly shifted its attention to its secondary goals of economic development and cultural exchange.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeisler1995167–168,_224–225-60) By the 1970s, the UN budget for social and economic development was far greater than its peacekeeping budget.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Post-Cold War (1991–1999)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Kofi_Annan_2012_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Kofi_Annan_2012_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kofi_Annan_2012_(cropped).jpg)

[Kofi Annan](/wiki/Kofi_Annan "Kofi Annan"), secretary-general from 1997 to 2006

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/UN_Members_Flags2.JPG/250px-UN_Members_Flags2.JPG)](/wiki/File:UN_Members_Flags2.JPG)

Flags of member nations at the [United Nations Headquarters](/wiki/Headquarters_of_the_United_Nations "Headquarters of the United Nations"), pictured in 2007

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/UN70.JPG/250px-UN70.JPG)](/wiki/File:UN70.JPG)

Marking of the UN's 70th anniversary – Budapest, 2015

After the Cold War, the UN saw a radical expansion in its peacekeeping duties, taking on more missions in five years than it had in the previous four decades.[[59]](#cite_note-61) Between 1988 and 2000, the number of adopted Security Council resolutions more than doubled, and the peacekeeping budget increased more than tenfold.[[60]](#cite_note-62)[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFasulo200443-63)[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeisler1995334-64)[[63]](#cite_note-65) The UN negotiated an end to the [Salvadoran Civil War](/wiki/Salvadoran_Civil_War "Salvadoran Civil War"), launched a successful [peacekeeping mission in Namibia](/wiki/United_Nations_Transition_Assistance_Group "United Nations Transition Assistance Group"), and oversaw democratic elections in post-[apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid_in_South_Africa "Apartheid in South Africa") South Africa and post-[Khmer Rouge](/wiki/Khmer_Rouge "Khmer Rouge") Cambodia.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeisler1995252–256-66) In 1991, the UN authorized a [US-led coalition](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War") that repulsed Iraq's [invasion of Kuwait](/wiki/Invasion_of_Kuwait "Invasion of Kuwait").[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeisler1995264–277-67) [Brian Urquhart](/wiki/Brian_Urquhart "Brian Urquhart"), the under-secretary-general of the UN from 1971 to 1985, later described the hopes raised by these successes as a "false renaissance" for the organization, given the more troubled missions that followed.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeisler1995334-64)

Beginning in the last decades of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"), critics of the UN condemned the organization for perceived mismanagement and corruption.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeisler1995226–227-68) In 1984, American president [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") withdrew the United States' funding from the [United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") (or UNESCO) over allegations of mismanagement, followed by the United Kingdom and Singapore.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeisler1995234–237-69)[[68]](#cite_note-70) [Boutros Boutros-Ghali](/wiki/Boutros_Boutros-Ghali "Boutros Boutros-Ghali"), the secretary-general from 1992 to 1996, initiated a reform of the Secretariat, somewhat reducing the size of the organization.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeisler1995285–286-71)[[70]](#cite_note-NST-72) His successor, [Kofi Annan](/wiki/Kofi_Annan "Kofi Annan"), initiated further management reforms in the face of threats from the US to withhold its UN dues.[[70]](#cite_note-NST-72)

Though the UN Charter had been written primarily to prevent aggression by one nation against another, in the early 1990s the UN faced several simultaneous, serious crises within Somalia, Haiti, Mozambique, and the nations that previously made up Yugoslavia.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy200766–67-73) The [UN mission in Somalia](/wiki/United_Nations_Operation_in_Somalia_II "United Nations Operation in Somalia II") was widely viewed as a failure after the United States' withdrawal following casualties in the [Battle of Mogadishu](/wiki/Battle_of_Mogadishu_(1993) "Battle of Mogadishu (1993)"). The [UN mission to Bosnia](/wiki/United_Nations_Protection_Force "United Nations Protection Force") faced worldwide ridicule for its indecisive and confused mission in the face of ethnic cleansing.[[72]](#cite_note-74) In 1994, the [UN Assistance Mission for Rwanda](/wiki/United_Nations_Assistance_Mission_for_Rwanda "United Nations Assistance Mission for Rwanda") failed to intervene in the [Rwandan genocide](/wiki/Rwandan_genocide "Rwandan genocide") amidst indecision in the Security Council.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy2007104-75)

From the late 1990s to the early 2000s, international interventions authorized by the UN took a wider variety of forms. The [United Nations Security Council Resolution 1244](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_Resolution_1244 "United Nations Security Council Resolution 1244") authorized the [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO")-led [Kosovo Force](/wiki/Kosovo_Force "Kosovo Force") beginning in 1999. The [UN mission](/wiki/United_Nations_Mission_in_Sierra_Leone "United Nations Mission in Sierra Leone") in the [Sierra Leone Civil War](/wiki/Sierra_Leone_Civil_War "Sierra Leone Civil War") was supplemented by a [British military intervention](/wiki/British_military_intervention_in_the_Sierra_Leone_Civil_War "British military intervention in the Sierra Leone Civil War"). The [invasion of Afghanistan in 2001](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%93present) "War in Afghanistan (2001–present)") was overseen by NATO.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy2007110–111-76) In 2003, the United States [invaded Iraq](/wiki/2003_invasion_of_Iraq "2003 invasion of Iraq") despite failing to pass a UN Security Council resolution for authorization, prompting a new round of questioning of the UN's effectiveness.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy2007111-77)

Under the eighth secretary-general, [Ban Ki-moon](/wiki/Ban_Ki-moon "Ban Ki-moon"), the UN intervened with peacekeepers in crises such as the [War in Darfur](/wiki/War_in_Darfur "War in Darfur") in Sudan and the [Kivu conflict](/wiki/Kivu_conflict "Kivu conflict") in the Democratic Republic of the Congo and sent observers and chemical weapons inspectors to the [Syrian Civil War](/wiki/Syrian_Civil_War "Syrian Civil War").[[76]](#cite_note-78) In 2013, [an internal review](/wiki/Secretary-General%27s_Internal_Review_Panel_on_United_Nations_Action_in_Sri_Lanka "Secretary-General's Internal Review Panel on United Nations Action in Sri Lanka") of UN actions in [the final battles](/wiki/Alleged_war_crimes_during_the_final_stages_of_the_Sri_Lankan_Civil_War "Alleged war crimes during the final stages of the Sri Lankan Civil War") of the [Sri Lankan Civil War](/wiki/Sri_Lankan_civil_war "Sri Lankan civil war") in 2009 concluded that the organization had suffered a "systemic failure".[[77]](#cite_note-79) In 2010, the organization suffered the worst loss of life in its history, when 101 personnel died in the [Haiti earthquake](/wiki/2010_Haiti_earthquake "2010 Haiti earthquake").[[78]](#cite_note-unmilestones2001to2010-80) Acting under the [United Nations Security Council Resolution 1973](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_Resolution_1973 "United Nations Security Council Resolution 1973") in 2011, [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") countries intervened in the [First Libyan Civil War](/wiki/First_Libyan_Civil_War "First Libyan Civil War").

The [Millennium Summit](/wiki/Millennium_Summit "Millennium Summit") was held in 2000 to discuss the UN's role in the 21st century.[[79]](#cite_note-BBCagenda-81) The three-day meeting was the largest gathering of world leaders in history, and it culminated in the adoption by all member states of the [Millennium Development Goals](/wiki/Millennium_Development_Goals "Millennium Development Goals") (or MDGs), a commitment to achieve international development in areas such as [poverty reduction](/wiki/Poverty_reduction "Poverty reduction"), [gender equality](/wiki/Gender_equality "Gender equality") and [public health](/wiki/Public_health "Public health"). Progress towards these goals, which were to be met by 2015, was ultimately uneven. The [2005 World Summit](/wiki/2005_World_Summit "2005 World Summit") reaffirmed the UN's focus on promoting development, peacekeeping, human rights and global security.[[80]](#cite_note-82) The [Sustainable Development Goals](/wiki/Sustainable_Development_Goals "Sustainable Development Goals") (or SDGs) were launched in 2015 to succeed the Millennium Development Goals.[[81]](#cite_note-Sustainable_Development_Goals-83)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/UN_Land_Cruiser._Dnipro.jpg/250px-UN_Land_Cruiser._Dnipro.jpg)](/wiki/File:UN_Land_Cruiser._Dnipro.jpg)

UN car in Dnipro, Ukraine

In addition to addressing global challenges, the UN has sought to improve its accountability and democratic legitimacy by engaging more with [civil society](/wiki/Civil_society "Civil society") and fostering a global constituency.[[82]](#cite_note-84) In an effort to enhance transparency, in 2016 the organization held its first public debate between candidates for secretary-general.[[83]](#cite_note-85) On 1 January 2017, Portuguese diplomat [António Guterres](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_Guterres "António Guterres"), who had previously served as the [UN High Commissioner for Refugees](/wiki/United_Nations_High_Commissioner_for_Refugees "United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees"), became the ninth secretary-general. Guterres has highlighted several key goals for his administration, including an emphasis on diplomacy for preventing conflicts, more effective peacekeeping efforts, and streamlining the organization to be more responsive and versatile to international needs.[[84]](#cite_note-86)

On 13 June 2019, the UN signed a Strategic Partnership Framework with the [World Economic Forum](/wiki/World_Economic_Forum "World Economic Forum") in order to "jointly accelerate" the implementation of the [2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development](/wiki/2030_Agenda_for_Sustainable_Development "2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development").[[85]](#cite_note-87)

### 2020s: financial crisis

Throughout most of its history, the UN has faced funding issues. However, they have become severe since 2020, and in 2025, the UN began facing a financial crisis resulting from delays in member state due payments and refusal to pay the amount the UN charges.[[86]](#cite_note-:2-88) A major problem is that the United States, the largest contributor, has a law in place since 1994 where it will not pay more than 25% of total UN Peacekeeping fees (although as of 2024 it was paying 27%). Additionally, the US and the second largest contributor, China, often delay their payments in order to influence the UN on topics such as the [Gaza war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war") and [Persecution of Uyghurs in China](/wiki/Persecution_of_Uyghurs_in_China "Persecution of Uyghurs in China"). Other countries began to follow suit, triggering a financial crisis.[[87]](#cite_note-89) On 19 May 2025, only 61 countries paid their dues on time and in full.[[86]](#cite_note-:2-88) The crisis is causing massive budget cuts within the UN, with the UN warning that millions of lives are being put at risk.[[88]](#cite_note-90)[[89]](#cite_note-91)[[86]](#cite_note-:2-88)[[90]](#cite_note-92) In January 2026, Guterres sent a letter to all members about an "imminent financial collapse" of the UN.[[91]](#cite_note-93)[[92]](#cite_note-94)[[93]](#cite_note-95)

## Structure

Main article: [United Nations System](/wiki/United_Nations_System "United Nations System")

The United Nations is part of the broader UN System, which includes an extensive network of institutions and entities. Central to the organization are five principal organs established by the UN Charter: the [General Assembly](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly "United Nations General Assembly"), the [Security Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council "United Nations Security Council"), the [Economic and Social Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Economic_and_Social_Council "United Nations Economic and Social Council"), the [International Court of Justice](/wiki/International_Court_of_Justice "International Court of Justice") and the [UN Secretariat](/wiki/United_Nations_Secretariat "United Nations Secretariat").[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFasulo20043–4-96) A sixth principal organ, the [Trusteeship Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Trusteeship_Council "United Nations Trusteeship Council"), suspended its operations on 1 November 1994 upon the independence of [Palau](/wiki/Palau "Palau"); the last remaining UN trustee territory.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFasulo20048-97)

Four of the five principal organs are located at the main UN Headquarters in New York City, while the International Court of Justice is seated in [The Hague](/wiki/The_Hague "The Hague").[[96]](#cite_note-98) Most other major agencies are based in the [UN offices at Geneva](/wiki/United_Nations_Office_at_Geneva "United Nations Office at Geneva"),[[97]](#cite_note-99) [Vienna](/wiki/United_Nations_Office_at_Vienna "United Nations Office at Vienna"),[[98]](#cite_note-100) and [Nairobi](/wiki/United_Nations_Office_at_Nairobi "United Nations Office at Nairobi"),[[99]](#cite_note-101) and additional UN institutions are located throughout the world. The six [official languages](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") of the UN, used in intergovernmental meetings and documents, are [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic"), [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"), [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"), [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language") and [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language").[[100]](#cite_note-langs-102) On the basis of the [Convention on the Privileges and Immunities of the United Nations](/wiki/Convention_on_the_Privileges_and_Immunities_of_the_United_Nations "Convention on the Privileges and Immunities of the United Nations"), the UN and its agencies are [immune](/wiki/Diplomatic_immunity "Diplomatic immunity") from the laws of the countries where they operate, safeguarding the UN's impartiality with regard to host and member countries.[[101]](#cite_note-103)

Below the six organs are, in the words of the author Linda Fasulo, "an amazing collection of entities and organizations, some of which are actually older than the UN itself and operate with almost complete independence from it".[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFasulo20044-104) These include specialized agencies, research and training institutions, programmes and funds and other UN entities.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFasulo20044–7-105)

All organizations in the UN system obey the *Noblemaire principle*, which calls for salaries that will attract and retain citizens of countries where compensation is highest, and which ensures equal pay for work of equal value regardless of the employee's nationality.[[104]](#cite_note-un-salaries-106)[[105]](#cite_note-107) In practice, the [International Civil Service Commission](/wiki/International_Civil_Service_Commission "International Civil Service Commission"), which governs the conditions of UN personnel, takes reference to the highest-paying national civil service.[[106]](#cite_note-108) Staff salaries are subject to an internal tax that is administered by the UN organizations.[[104]](#cite_note-un-salaries-106)[[107]](#cite_note-109)

### General Assembly

Main article: [United Nations General Assembly](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly "United Nations General Assembly")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/RIAN_archive_828797_Mikhail_Gorbachev_addressing_UN_General_Assembly_session.jpg/250px-RIAN_archive_828797_Mikhail_Gorbachev_addressing_UN_General_Assembly_session.jpg)](/wiki/File:RIAN_archive_828797_Mikhail_Gorbachev_addressing_UN_General_Assembly_session.jpg)

[Mikhail Gorbachev](/wiki/Mikhail_Gorbachev "Mikhail Gorbachev"), leader of the Soviet Union, addressing the UN General Assembly in December 1988

The General Assembly is the primary [deliberative assembly](/wiki/Deliberative_assembly "Deliberative assembly") of the UN. Composed of all [UN member states](/wiki/Member_states_of_the_United_Nations "Member states of the United Nations"), the assembly gathers at annual sessions at the [General Assembly Hall](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly_Hall "United Nations General Assembly Hall"), but emergency sessions can be summoned.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFomerand2009131–133-111) The assembly is led by a [president](/wiki/President_of_the_United_Nations_General_Assembly "President of the United Nations General Assembly"), elected by the member states on a rotating regional basis, and 21 vice-presidents.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFasulo200469–70-112) The first session convened on 10 January 1946 in the [Methodist Central Hall](/wiki/Methodist_Central_Hall,_Westminster "Methodist Central Hall, Westminster") in London and comprised representatives of 51 nations.[[34]](#cite_note-unmilestones1941to1950-35)

When the General Assembly decides on seminal questions such as those on peace and security, admission of new members and budgetary matters, a two-thirds majority of those present and voting is required.[[111]](#cite_note-113)[[112]](#cite_note-114) All other questions are decided by a majority vote. Each member has one vote. Apart from the approval of budgetary matters, resolutions are not binding on the members. The Assembly may make recommendations on any matters within the scope of the UN, except matters of peace and security that are under consideration by the Security Council.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFomerand2009131–133-111)

Draft resolutions can be forwarded to the General Assembly by its six main committees:[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFasulo200470–73-115)

* [First Committee (Disarmament and International Security)](/wiki/General_Assembly_First_Committee "General Assembly First Committee")
* [Second Committee (Economic and Financial)](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly_Second_Committee "United Nations General Assembly Second Committee")
* [Third Committee (Social, Humanitarian, and Cultural)](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly_Third_Committee "United Nations General Assembly Third Committee")
* [Fourth Committee (Special Political and Decolonization)](/wiki/United_Nations_Fourth_Committee "United Nations Fourth Committee")
* [Fifth Committee (Administrative and Budgetary)](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly_Fifth_Committee "United Nations General Assembly Fifth Committee")
* [Sixth Committee (Legal)](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly_Sixth_Committee_(Legal) "United Nations General Assembly Sixth Committee (Legal)")

As well as by the following two committees:

* [General Committee](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Committee "United Nations General Committee") – a supervisory committee consisting of the assembly's president, vice-presidents, and committee heads
* [Credentials Committee](/wiki/United_Nations_Credentials_Committee "United Nations Credentials Committee") – responsible for determining the credentials of each member nation's UN representatives

### Security Council

Main article: [United Nations Security Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council "United Nations Security Council")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Colin_Powell_anthrax_vial._5_Feb_2003_at_the_UN.jpg/250px-Colin_Powell_anthrax_vial._5_Feb_2003_at_the_UN.jpg)](/wiki/File:Colin_Powell_anthrax_vial._5_Feb_2003_at_the_UN.jpg)

[Colin Powell](/wiki/Colin_Powell "Colin Powell"), the [US Secretary of State](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_State "United States Secretary of State"), demonstrates a vial with [alleged](/wiki/Legitimacy_of_the_2003_invasion_of_Iraq#Weapons_of_Mass_Destruction "Legitimacy of the 2003 invasion of Iraq") Iraq [chemical weapon](/wiki/Chemical_weapon "Chemical weapon") probes to the UN Security Council on [Iraq war](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_and_the_Iraq_War "United Nations Security Council and the Iraq War") hearings, 5 February 2003.

The Security Council is charged with maintaining peace and security among nations. While other organs of the UN can only make recommendations to member states, the Security Council has the power to make binding decisions that member states have agreed to carry out, under the terms of Charter Article 25.[[114]](#cite_note-Chapter|V-116) The decisions of the council are known as [United Nations Security Council resolutions](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_resolution "United Nations Security Council resolution").[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFasulo200439–43-117)

The Security Council is made up of fifteen member states: five permanent members (China, France, Russia, the United Kingdom and the United States) and ten non-permanent members ([Algeria](/wiki/Algeria "Algeria"), [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark"), [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece"), [Guyana](/wiki/Guyana "Guyana"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), [Panama](/wiki/Panama "Panama"), the [Republic of Korea](/wiki/Republic_of_Korea "Republic of Korea"), [Sierra Leone](/wiki/Sierra_Leone "Sierra Leone"), [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia"), and [Somalia](/wiki/Somalia "Somalia"), as of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=United_Nations&action=edit)). The five permanent members hold [veto power](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_veto_power "United Nations Security Council veto power") over UN resolutions, allowing a permanent member to block adoption of a resolution, though not debate. The ten temporary seats are held for two-year terms, with five members elected each year by the General Assembly on a [regional basis](/wiki/United_Nations_Regional_Groups "United Nations Regional Groups").[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFasulo200440–41-118) The presidency of the Security Council rotates alphabetically each month.[[117]](#cite_note-119)

### UN Secretariat

Main articles: [United Nations Secretariat](/wiki/United_Nations_Secretariat "United Nations Secretariat") and [Secretary-General of the United Nations](/wiki/Secretary-General_of_the_United_Nations "Secretary-General of the United Nations")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Ant%C3%B3nio_Guterres%2C_23.03.23.jpg/250px-Ant%C3%B3nio_Guterres%2C_23.03.23.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ant%C3%B3nio_Guterres,_23.03.23.jpg)

[António Guterres](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_Guterres "António Guterres"), the current [secretary-general](/wiki/Secretary-General_of_the_United_Nations "Secretary-General of the United Nations")

The UN Secretariat carries out the day-to-day duties required to operate and maintain the UN system.[[118]](#cite_note-120) It is composed of tens of thousands of international civil servants worldwide and headed by the [secretary-general](/wiki/Secretary-General_of_the_United_Nations "Secretary-General of the United Nations"), who is assisted by the [deputy secretary-general](/wiki/Deputy_Secretary-General_of_the_United_Nations "Deputy Secretary-General of the United Nations").[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFasulo200421-121) The Secretariat's duties include providing information and facilities needed by UN bodies for their meetings and carrying out tasks as directed by the Security Council, the General Assembly, the Economic and Social Council, and other UN bodies.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFomerand2009285-122)

The secretary-general acts as the spokesperson and leader of the UN. The position is defined in the UN Charter as the organization's chief administrative officer.[[121]](#cite_note-123) Article 99 of the charter states that the secretary-general can bring to the Security Council's attention "any matter which in their opinion may threaten the maintenance of international peace and security", a phrase that secretaries-general since [Trygve Lie](/wiki/Trygve_Lie "Trygve Lie") have interpreted as giving the position broad scope for action on the world stage.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeisler199531–32-124) The office has evolved into a dual role of an administrator of the UN organization and a diplomat and mediator addressing disputes between member states and finding consensus to [global issues](/wiki/List_of_global_issues "List of global issues").[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy200759–62-125)

The secretary-general is appointed by the General Assembly, after being recommended by the Security Council, where the permanent members have veto power. There are no specific criteria for the post, but over the years it has become accepted that the position shall be held for one or two terms of five years.[[124]](#cite_note-126) The current secretary-general is [António Guterres](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_Guterres "António Guterres") of Portugal, who replaced [Ban Ki-moon](/wiki/Ban_Ki-moon "Ban Ki-moon") in 2017.

### International Court of Justice

Main article: [International Court of Justice](/wiki/International_Court_of_Justice "International Court of Justice")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Grand_Hall_de_Justice_de_Palais_de_La_Paix_%C3%A0_La_Haye_Pays-Bas.jpg/250px-Grand_Hall_de_Justice_de_Palais_de_La_Paix_%C3%A0_La_Haye_Pays-Bas.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grand_Hall_de_Justice_de_Palais_de_La_Paix_%C3%A0_La_Haye_Pays-Bas.jpg)

The ICJ ruled that [Kosovo's unilateral declaration of independence](/wiki/2008_Kosovo_declaration_of_independence "2008 Kosovo declaration of independence") from Serbia in 2008 did not violate international law.

The International Court of Justice (or ICJ), sometimes known as the World Court,[[126]](#cite_note-britannica.com-128) is the primary judicial organ of the UN. It is the successor to the [Permanent Court of International Justice](/wiki/Permanent_Court_of_International_Justice "Permanent Court of International Justice") and occupies the body's former headquarters in the [Peace Palace](/wiki/Peace_Palace "Peace Palace") in [The Hague, Netherlands](/wiki/The_Hague "The Hague"), making it the only principal organ not based in New York City. The ICJ's main function is adjudicating disputes among nations. Examples of issues they have heard include war crimes, violations of state sovereignty and ethnic cleansing.[[127]](#cite_note-129) The court can also be called upon by other UN organs to provide advisory opinions on matters of international law.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFomerand2009183-130) All UN member states are parties to the [ICJ Statute](/wiki/Statute_of_the_International_Court_of_Justice "Statute of the International Court of Justice"), which forms an integral part of the UN Charter, and non-members may also become parties. The ICJ's rulings are binding upon parties and, along with its advisory opinions, serve as [sources of international law](/wiki/Sources_of_international_law "Sources of international law").[[126]](#cite_note-britannica.com-128) The court is composed of 15 judges appointed to nine-year terms by the General Assembly. Every sitting judge must be from a different nation.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFomerand2009183-130)[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFasulo2004100–101-131)

### Economic and Social Council

Main article: [United Nations Economic and Social Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Economic_and_Social_Council "United Nations Economic and Social Council")

The Economic and Social Council (or the ECOSOC) assists the General Assembly in promoting international economic and social co-operation and development.[[130]](#cite_note-:1-132) It was established to serve as the UN's primary forum for global issues and is the largest and most complex UN body.[[130]](#cite_note-:1-132) The ECOSOC's functions include gathering data, conducting studies and advising and making recommendations to member states.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFomerand2009103–104-133)[[132]](#cite_note-134) Its work is carried out primarily by subsidiary bodies focused on a wide variety of topics. These include the [United Nations Permanent Forum on Indigenous Issues](/wiki/United_Nations_Permanent_Forum_on_Indigenous_Issues "United Nations Permanent Forum on Indigenous Issues"), which advises UN agencies on issues relating to [indigenous peoples](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples "Indigenous peoples"), the [United Nations Forum on Forests](/wiki/United_Nations_Forum_on_Forests "United Nations Forum on Forests"), which coordinates and promotes sustainable forest management, the [United Nations Statistical Commission](/wiki/United_Nations_Statistical_Commission "United Nations Statistical Commission"), which co-ordinates information-gathering efforts between agencies, and the [Commission on Sustainable Development](/wiki/Commission_on_Sustainable_Development "Commission on Sustainable Development"), which co-ordinates efforts between UN agencies and NGOs working towards [sustainable development](/wiki/Sustainable_development "Sustainable development"). ECOSOC may also grant consultative status to non-governmental organizations.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFomerand2009103–104-133) As of April 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=United_Nations&action=edit) almost 5,600 organizations have this status.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFasulo2004156-135)[[134]](#cite_note-136)

### Specialized agencies

Main article: [List of specialized agencies of the United Nations](/wiki/List_of_specialized_agencies_of_the_United_Nations "List of specialized agencies of the United Nations")

The UN Charter stipulates that each primary organ of the United Nations can establish various specialized agencies to fulfill its duties.[[135]](#cite_note-CharterIX-137) Specialized agencies are autonomous organizations working with the United Nations and each other through the coordinating machinery of the Economic and Social Council. Each was integrated into the UN system through an agreement with the UN under UN Charter article 57.[[136]](#cite_note-CEB-138) There are fifteen specialized agencies, which perform functions as diverse as facilitating international travel, preventing and addressing pandemics, and promoting economic development.[[137]](#cite_note-139)[[c]](#cite_note-140)

### Funds, programmes, and other bodies

The United Nations system includes a myriad of autonomous, separately administered [funds, programmes, research and training institutes, and other subsidiary bodies](/wiki/United_Nations_System#Funds_and_programmes,_research_and_training_institutes,_and_other_bodies "United Nations System").[[138]](#cite_note-un.org-141) Each of these entities have their own area of work, governance structure, and budgets such as the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") (or the WTO) and the [International Atomic Energy Agency](/wiki/International_Atomic_Energy_Agency "International Atomic Energy Agency") (or the IAEA), operate independently of the UN but maintain formal partnership agreements. The UN performs much of its humanitarian work through these institutions, such as preventing famine and malnutrition (the [World Food Programme](/wiki/World_Food_Programme "World Food Programme")), protecting vulnerable and displaced people (the [UNHCR](/wiki/United_Nations_High_Commissioner_for_Refugees "United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees")), and combating the HIV/AIDS pandemic (the [UNAIDS](/wiki/Joint_United_Nations_Programme_on_HIV/AIDS "Joint United Nations Programme on HIV/AIDS")).[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFasulo2004171–177-142)
