## Membership

Main articles: [Member states of the United Nations](/wiki/Member_states_of_the_United_Nations "Member states of the United Nations") and [Expulsion from the United Nations](/wiki/Expulsion_from_the_United_Nations "Expulsion from the United Nations")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/United_Nations_%28Member_States_and_Territories%29.svg/500px-United_Nations_%28Member_States_and_Territories%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:United_Nations_(Member_States_and_Territories).svg)

193 [UN member states](/wiki/Member_states_of_the_United_Nations "Member states of the United Nations")

2 [UN Observer States](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly_observers "United Nations General Assembly observers") (Palestine, Holy See)

2 eligible Non-Member States (Niue, Cook Islands)

17 [non-self-governing territories](/wiki/United_Nations_list_of_non-self-governing_territories "United Nations list of non-self-governing territories")

[Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica")

All the world's [undisputed](/wiki/List_of_states_with_limited_recognition "List of states with limited recognition") [independent states](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states "List of sovereign states") are members of the United Nations.[[140]](#cite_note-UN_SouthSudan_193rd_state-143) [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan"), which joined 14 July 2011, is the most recent addition, bringing a total of 193 UN member states.[[2]](#cite_note-members-2) The UN Charter outlines the membership rules:

> 1. Membership in the United Nations is open to all other peace-loving states that accept the obligations contained in the present Charter and, in the judgment of the Organization, are able and willing to carry out these obligations.
> 2. The admission of any such state to membership in the United Nations will be effected by a decision of the General Assembly upon the recommendation of the Security Council. Chapter II, Article 4.[[141]](#cite_note-144)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Presiden_Sukarno_%28retouched%29.jpg/250px-Presiden_Sukarno_%28retouched%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Presiden_Sukarno_(retouched).jpg)

Under the leadership of [Sukarno](/wiki/Sukarno "Sukarno"), Indonesia was [the first and only country that attempted](/wiki/Indonesia_and_the_United_Nations#Withdrawal_in_1965 "Indonesia and the United Nations") to leave the United Nations.

In addition, there are two [non-member observer states](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly_observers "United Nations General Assembly observers"): the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") and [Palestine](/wiki/Palestine "Palestine").[[3]](#cite_note-Non-member_States-3)[[d]](#cite_note-145) The [Cook Islands](/wiki/Cook_Islands "Cook Islands") and [Niue](/wiki/Niue "Niue"), both [states in free association](/wiki/Associated_state "Associated state") with [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), are full members of several UN specialized agencies and have had their "full treaty-making capacity" recognized by the Secretariat.[[142]](#cite_note-art102-146)

[Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") was the first and the only nation that attempted to withdraw its membership from the United Nations, in protest to the election of Malaysia as a non-permanent member of the [Security Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council "United Nations Security Council") in 1965 during [conflict between the two countries](/wiki/Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation "Indonesia–Malaysia confrontation").[[143]](#cite_note-147) After forming [CONEFO](/wiki/CONEFO "CONEFO") as a short-lived rival to the UN, Indonesia resumed its membership in 1966.

### Group of 77

Main article: [Group of 77](/wiki/Group_of_77 "Group of 77")

The Group of 77 (or the G77) at the UN is a loose coalition of [developing nations](/wiki/Developing_nations "Developing nations") that is designed to promote its members' collective economic interests and create an enhanced joint negotiating capacity in the UN. Seventy-seven nations founded the organization, but by November 2013 the organization had since expanded to 133 member countries.[[144]](#cite_note-148) The group was founded 15 June 1964 by the "Joint Declaration of the Seventy-Seven Countries" issued at the [United Nations Conference on Trade and Development](/wiki/United_Nations_Conference_on_Trade_and_Development "United Nations Conference on Trade and Development") (or the UNCTAD). The group held its first major meeting in [Algiers](/wiki/Algiers "Algiers") in 1967, where it adopted the Charter of Algiers and established the basis for permanent institutional structures.[[145]](#cite_note-149) With the adoption of the [New International Economic Order](/wiki/New_International_Economic_Order "New International Economic Order") by developing countries in the 1970s, the work of the G77 spread throughout the UN system.[[146]](#cite_note-150) Similar groupings of developing states also operate in other UN agencies, such as the [Group of 24](/wiki/Group_of_24 "Group of 24") (or the G-24), which operates in the IMF on monetary affairs.[[147]](#cite_note-151)

## Objectives

The overarching strategy of the United Nations is captured in the [United Nations Common Agenda](/wiki/United_Nations_Common_Agenda "United Nations Common Agenda").[[148]](#cite_note-152)[[149]](#cite_note-153)

### Peacekeeping and security

Main article: [United Nations peacekeeping](/wiki/United_Nations_peacekeeping "United Nations peacekeeping")

See also: [List of United Nations peacekeeping missions](/wiki/List_of_United_Nations_peacekeeping_missions "List of United Nations peacekeeping missions")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/United_Nations_Peacekeeping_Helmet_Icon.svg/250px-United_Nations_Peacekeeping_Helmet_Icon.svg.png)](/wiki/File:United_Nations_Peacekeeping_Helmet_Icon.svg)

United Nations Peacekeeping Logo

The UN, after approval by the Security Council, sends peacekeepers to regions where armed conflict has recently ceased or paused to enforce the terms of peace agreements and to discourage combatants from resuming hostilities. Since the UN does not maintain its own military, peacekeeping forces are voluntarily provided by member states. These soldiers are sometimes nicknamed "Blue Helmets" because they wear distinctive blue helmets.[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFasulo200452-154)[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoulon1998ix-155) Peacekeeping forces as a whole received the Nobel Peace Prize in 1988.[[152]](#cite_note-156)

The UN has carried out 71 peacekeeping operations since 1947, and As of April 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=United_Nations&action=edit), over 88,000 peacekeeping personnel from 121 nations have been deployed on missions.[[153]](#cite_note-157) The largest is the [United Nations Mission in South Sudan](/wiki/United_Nations_Mission_in_South_Sudan "United Nations Mission in South Sudan") (or UNMISS), which has close to 19,200 uniformed personnel,[[154]](#cite_note-158) and the smallest, the [United Nations Military Observer Group in India and Pakistan](/wiki/United_Nations_Military_Observer_Group_in_India_and_Pakistan "United Nations Military Observer Group in India and Pakistan") (or UNMOGIP), consists of 113 civilians and experts charged with monitoring the ceasefire in [Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(state) "Jammu and Kashmir (state)"). UN peacekeepers with the [United Nations Truce Supervision Organization](/wiki/United_Nations_Truce_Supervision_Organization "United Nations Truce Supervision Organization") (or UNTSO) have been stationed in the Middle East since 1948, the longest-running active peacekeeping mission.[[155]](#cite_note-UNPO-159)

A study by the RAND Corporation in 2005 found the UN to be successful in two-thirds of its peacekeeping efforts. It compared efforts at nation-building by the UN to those of the United States and found that 87.5% of UN cases are at peace, as compared with 50% of U.S. cases at peace.[[156]](#cite_note-160) Also in 2005, the [Human Security Report](/wiki/Human_Security_Report_2005 "Human Security Report 2005") documented a decline in the number of wars, [genocides](/wiki/Genocide "Genocide"), and [human rights abuses](/wiki/Human_rights_abuses "Human rights abuses") since the end of the Cold War, and presented evidence, albeit circumstantial, that international activism – mostly spearheaded by the UN – has been the main cause of the decline in armed conflict.[[157]](#cite_note-161) Situations in which the UN has not only acted to keep the peace but also intervened include the Korean War and the authorization of intervention in Iraq after the Gulf War.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy200756-162) Further studies published between 2008 and 2021 determined UN peacekeeping operations to be more effective at ensuring long-lasting peace and minimizing civilian casualties.[[159]](#cite_note-:0-163)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/GreenLine_BufferZone_Large.JPG/250px-GreenLine_BufferZone_Large.JPG)](/wiki/File:GreenLine_BufferZone_Large.JPG)

The [UN Buffer Zone in Cyprus](/wiki/UN_Buffer_Zone_in_Cyprus "UN Buffer Zone in Cyprus") was established in 1974 following the [Turkish invasion of Cyprus](/wiki/Turkish_invasion_of_Cyprus "Turkish invasion of Cyprus").

The UN has also drawn criticism for perceived failures. In many cases, member states have shown reluctance to achieve or enforce Security Council resolutions. Disagreements in the Security Council about military action and intervention are seen as having failed to prevent the [Bangladesh genocide in 1971](/wiki/1971_Bangladesh_Genocide "1971 Bangladesh Genocide"),[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBall201146-164) the [Cambodian genocide](/wiki/Khmer_Rouge_rule_of_Cambodia "Khmer Rouge rule of Cambodia") in the 1970s,[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy2007187-165) and the Rwandan genocide in 1994.[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy2007102–105-166) Similarly, UN inaction is blamed for failing to either prevent the [Srebrenica massacre](/wiki/Srebrenica_massacre "Srebrenica massacre") or complete the peacekeeping operations during the [Somali Civil War](/wiki/Somali_Civil_War "Somali Civil War").[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeisler1995294–311-167) UN peacekeepers have also been accused of child rape, soliciting prostitutes, and sexual abuse during various peacekeeping missions in the Democratic Republic of the Congo,[[164]](#cite_note-168) Haiti,[[165]](#cite_note-169) Liberia,[[166]](#cite_note-170) Sudan,[[167]](#cite_note-171) Burundi, and Côte d'Ivoire.[[168]](#cite_note-172) Scientists cited UN peacekeepers from Nepal as the source of the [2010s Haiti cholera outbreak](/wiki/2010s_Haiti_cholera_outbreak "2010s Haiti cholera outbreak"), which killed more than 8,000 people.[[169]](#cite_note-173)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/A_United_Nations_Peacekeeper_from_Nepal_provides_security_at_a_rice_distribution_site_in_Kenscoff%2C_Haiti%2C_Feb._20%2C_2010_100220-N-HX866-010.jpg/250px-A_United_Nations_Peacekeeper_from_Nepal_provides_security_at_a_rice_distribution_site_in_Kenscoff%2C_Haiti%2C_Feb._20%2C_2010_100220-N-HX866-010.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_United_Nations_Peacekeeper_from_Nepal_provides_security_at_a_rice_distribution_site_in_Kenscoff,_Haiti,_Feb._20,_2010_100220-N-HX866-010.jpg)

A Nepalese soldier on a peacekeeping deployment providing security at a rice distribution site in Haiti during 2010

In addition to peacekeeping, the UN is also active in encouraging [disarmament](/wiki/Disarmament "Disarmament"). Regulation of armaments was included in the writing of the UN Charter in 1945 and was envisioned as a way of limiting the use of human and economic resources for their creation.[[114]](#cite_note-Chapter|V-116) The advent of [nuclear weapons](/wiki/Nuclear_weapon "Nuclear weapon") came only weeks after the signing of the charter, resulting in the first [resolution](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly_resolution "United Nations General Assembly resolution") of the first [General Assembly](/wiki/General_Assembly "General Assembly") meeting calling for specific proposals for "the elimination from national armaments of atomic weapons and of all other major weapons adaptable to mass destruction".[[170]](#cite_note-174) The UN has been involved with arms-limitation treaties such as the [Outer Space Treaty](/wiki/Outer_Space_Treaty "Outer Space Treaty"), the [Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Non-Proliferation_of_Nuclear_Weapons "Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons"), the [Seabed Arms Control Treaty](/wiki/Seabed_Arms_Control_Treaty "Seabed Arms Control Treaty"), the [Biological Weapons Convention](/wiki/Biological_Weapons_Convention "Biological Weapons Convention"), the [Chemical Weapons Convention](/wiki/Chemical_Weapons_Convention "Chemical Weapons Convention"), and the [Ottawa Treaty](/wiki/Ottawa_Treaty "Ottawa Treaty").[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFasulo2004188–189-175) Three UN bodies oversee arms proliferation issues: the [International Atomic Energy Agency](/wiki/International_Atomic_Energy_Agency "International Atomic Energy Agency"), the [Organisation for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons](/wiki/Organisation_for_the_Prohibition_of_Chemical_Weapons "Organisation for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons") and the [Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty Organization Preparatory Commission](/wiki/Comprehensive_Nuclear-Test-Ban_Treaty_Organization_Preparatory_Commission "Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty Organization Preparatory Commission").[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFasulo2004189–190-176) Additionally, many peacekeeping missions focus on disarmament: several operations in West Africa disarmed roughly 250,000 former combatants and secured tens of thousands of weapons and millions of munitions.[[173]](#cite_note-177)

### Human rights

One of the UN's primary purposes is "promoting and encouraging respect for human rights and for fundamental freedoms for all without distinction as to race, sex, language, or religion", and member states pledge to undertake "joint and separate action" to protect these rights.[[135]](#cite_note-CharterIX-137)[[174]](#cite_note-Charter1-178)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Eleanor_Roosevelt_UDHR.jpg/250px-Eleanor_Roosevelt_UDHR.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eleanor_Roosevelt_UDHR.jpg)

[Eleanor Roosevelt](/wiki/Eleanor_Roosevelt "Eleanor Roosevelt") with the [Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "Universal Declaration of Human Rights"), 1949

In 1948, the General Assembly adopted a [Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "Universal Declaration of Human Rights"), drafted by a committee headed by American diplomat and activist [Eleanor Roosevelt](/wiki/Eleanor_Roosevelt "Eleanor Roosevelt"), and including the French lawyer [René Cassin](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Cassin "René Cassin"). The document proclaims basic civil, political and economic rights common to all human beings, though its effectiveness towards achieving these ends has been disputed since its drafting.[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy2007178–182-179) The Declaration serves as a "common standard of achievement for all people and all nations" rather than a legally binding document, but it has become the basis of two binding treaties, the [International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights](/wiki/International_Covenant_on_Civil_and_Political_Rights "International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights") and the [International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights](/wiki/International_Covenant_on_Economic,_Social_and_Cultural_Rights "International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights").[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFomerand2009377-180) In practice, the UN is unable to take significant action against human rights abuses without a Security Council resolution, though it does substantial work in investigating and reporting abuses.[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy2007185,_188-181)

In 1979, the General Assembly adopted the [Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination against Women](/wiki/Convention_on_the_Elimination_of_All_Forms_of_Discrimination_against_Women "Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination against Women"); followed by the [Convention on the Rights of the Child](/wiki/Convention_on_the_Rights_of_the_Child "Convention on the Rights of the Child") in 1989.[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFomerand200970,_73-182) With the end of the Cold War, the push for human rights action took on new impetus.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy2007192-183) The [United Nations Commission on Human Rights](/wiki/United_Nations_Commission_on_Human_Rights "United Nations Commission on Human Rights") was formed in 1993 to oversee human rights issues for the UN, following the recommendation of that year's [World Conference on Human Rights](/wiki/World_Conference_on_Human_Rights "World Conference on Human Rights"). Jacques Fomerand, a scholar of the UN, describes the organization's mandate as "broad and vague", with only "meagre" resources to carry it out.[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFomerand2009347-184) In 2006, it was replaced by a [Human Rights Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Human_Rights_Council "United Nations Human Rights Council") consisting of 47 nations.[[181]](#cite_note-185) Also in 2006, the General Assembly passed a [Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous People](/wiki/Declaration_on_the_Rights_of_Indigenous_Peoples "Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples"),[[182]](#cite_note-186) and in 2011 it passed its first resolution recognizing the rights of members of the [LGBTQ+](/wiki/LGBT "LGBT") community.[[183]](#cite_note-187)

Other UN bodies responsible for [women's rights](/wiki/Women%27s_rights "Women's rights") issues include the [United Nations Commission on the Status of Women](/wiki/United_Nations_Commission_on_the_Status_of_Women "United Nations Commission on the Status of Women"), the [United Nations Development Fund for Women](/wiki/United_Nations_Development_Fund_for_Women "United Nations Development Fund for Women") and the [United Nations International Research and Training Institute for the Advancement of Women](/wiki/United_Nations_International_Research_and_Training_Institute_for_the_Advancement_of_Women "United Nations International Research and Training Institute for the Advancement of Women").[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFomerand200957,_194,_341-188) The UN Permanent Forum on Indigenous Issues, one of three bodies with a mandate to oversee issues related to indigenous peoples, held its first session in 2002.[[185]](#cite_note-189)

### Economic development and humanitarian assistance

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Millennium Development Goals[[186]](#cite_note-190) | |
| 1. Eradicate extreme poverty and hunger 2. Achieve universal primary education 3. Promote gender equality and empower women 4. Reduce child mortality 5. Improve maternal health 6. Combat HIV/AIDS, malaria, and other diseases 7. Ensure environmental sustainability 8. Develop a global partnership for development |

Another primary purpose of the UN is "to achieve international co-operation in solving international problems of an economic, social, cultural and humanitarian character".[[174]](#cite_note-Charter1-178) Numerous bodies have been created to work towards this goal, primarily under the authority of the General Assembly and the ECOSOC.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy2007143–144-191) In 2000, the 192 UN member states agreed to achieve eight [Millennium Development Goals](/wiki/Millennium_Development_Goals "Millennium Development Goals") by 2015.[[188]](#cite_note-192) The [Sustainable Development Goals](/wiki/Sustainable_Development_Goals "Sustainable Development Goals") were launched in 2015 to succeed the Millennium Development Goals.[[81]](#cite_note-Sustainable_Development_Goals-83) The SDGs have an associated financing framework called the [Addis Ababa Action Agenda](/wiki/Addis_Ababa_Action_Agenda "Addis Ababa Action Agenda").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Directors_of_Global_Smallpox_Eradication_Program.jpg/250px-Directors_of_Global_Smallpox_Eradication_Program.jpg)](/wiki/File:Directors_of_Global_Smallpox_Eradication_Program.jpg)

Three former directors of the [Global Smallpox Eradication Programme](/wiki/Smallpox#Eradication "Smallpox") reading the news that smallpox has been globally eradicated in 1980

The [UN Development Programme](/wiki/United_Nations_Development_Programme "United Nations Development Programme") (or the UNDP), an organization for grant-based technical assistance, is one of the leading bodies in the field of [international development](/wiki/International_development "International development"). The organization also publishes the UN [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index"), a comparative measure [ranking countries](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_Human_Development_Index "List of countries by Human Development Index") by poverty, literacy, education, life expectancy, and other factors.[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFasulo2004169–170,_172-193)[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFomerand2009341–342-194) The [Food and Agriculture Organization](/wiki/Food_and_Agriculture_Organization "Food and Agriculture Organization") (or the FAO) promotes agricultural development and food security.[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFomerand2009126-195) The [United Nations Children's Fund](/wiki/UNICEF "UNICEF") (or UNICEF) was created in 1946 to aid European children after the Second World War and expanded its mission to provide aid around the world and to uphold the convention on the Rights of the Child.[[192]](#cite_note-196)[[193]](#cite_note-197)

The [World Bank Group](/wiki/World_Bank_Group "World Bank Group") and the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund") (or the IMF) are independent, specialized agencies and observers within the UN framework.[[194]](#cite_note-198) They were initially formed separately from the UN through the [Bretton Woods Agreement](/wiki/Bretton_Woods_system "Bretton Woods system").[[195]](#cite_note-199) The World Bank provides loans for international development, while the IMF promotes international economic co-operation and gives emergency loans to indebted countries.[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFomerand2009175,_191–192-200)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Iman_Mutlaq_visits_Zaatari_Refugee_Camp.jpg/250px-Iman_Mutlaq_visits_Zaatari_Refugee_Camp.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iman_Mutlaq_visits_Zaatari_Refugee_Camp.jpg)

In [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan"), UNHCR remains responsible for the [Syrian refugees](/wiki/Syrian_refugees "Syrian refugees") and the [Zaatari refugee camp](/wiki/Zaatari_refugee_camp "Zaatari refugee camp").

The [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") (or WHO), which focuses on international health issues and disease eradication, is another of the UN's largest agencies. In 1980, the agency announced that the eradication of [smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox") had been completed. In subsequent decades, [WHO](/wiki/WHO "WHO") eradicated [polio](/wiki/Polio "Polio"), [river blindness](/wiki/River_blindness "River blindness"), and [leprosy](/wiki/Leprosy "Leprosy").[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFasulo2004176–177-201) The [Joint United Nations Programme on HIV/AIDS](/wiki/Joint_United_Nations_Programme_on_HIV/AIDS "Joint United Nations Programme on HIV/AIDS") (or UNAIDS) coordinated the organization's response to the AIDS epidemic.[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFomerand2009199–200-202) The [UN Population Fund](/wiki/UN_Population_Fund "UN Population Fund"), which also dedicates part of its resources to combating HIV, is the world's largest source of funding for [reproductive health](/wiki/Reproductive_health "Reproductive health") and [family planning](/wiki/Family_planning "Family planning") services.[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFomerand2009368-203)

Along with the [International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement](/wiki/International_Red_Cross_and_Red_Crescent_Movement "International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement"), the UN takes a leading role in coordinating emergency relief.[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFasulo2004183-204) The [World Food Programme](/wiki/World_Food_Programme "World Food Programme") (or the WFP) provides food aid in response to famine, natural disasters, and armed conflict. The organization feeds an average of 90 million people in 80 nations per year.[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFasulo2004183-204)[[201]](#cite_note-205) The [Office of the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees](/wiki/Office_of_the_United_Nations_High_Commissioner_for_Refugees "Office of the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees") (or the UNHCR) works to protect the rights of refugees, asylum seekers and stateless people.[[202]](#cite_note-206) The UNHCR and the WFP programmes are funded by voluntary contributions from governments, corporations, and individuals, though the UNHCR's administrative costs are paid for by the UN's primary budget.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFomerand2009348,_398-207)

### Environment and climate

Further information: [United Nations Environment Programme](/wiki/United_Nations_Environment_Programme "United Nations Environment Programme"), [United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change](/wiki/United_Nations_Framework_Convention_on_Climate_Change "United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change"), [Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change](/wiki/Intergovernmental_Panel_on_Climate_Change "Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change"), and [Youth Advisory Group on Climate Change](/wiki/Youth_Advisory_Group_on_Climate_Change "Youth Advisory Group on Climate Change")

Beginning with the formation of the [UN Environmental Programme](/wiki/UN_Environmental_Programme "UN Environmental Programme") (or the UNEP) in 1972, the UN has made environmental issues a prominent part of its agenda. A lack of success in the first two decades of UN work in this area led to the [Earth Summit](/wiki/Earth_Summit "Earth Summit") in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil, in 1992, which sought to give new impetus to these efforts.[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy2007160–162-208) In 1988, the UNEP and the [World Meteorological Organization](/wiki/World_Meteorological_Organization "World Meteorological Organization") (or the WMO), another UN organization, established the [Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change](/wiki/Intergovernmental_Panel_on_Climate_Change "Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change"), which assesses and reports on research on [global warming](/wiki/Global_warming "Global warming").[[205]](#cite_note-209) The UN-sponsored [Kyoto Protocol](/wiki/Kyoto_Protocol "Kyoto Protocol") set legally binding emissions reduction targets for ratifying states.[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFasulo2004179-210)

### Other global issues

Since the UN's creation, over 80 colonies have attained independence. The General Assembly adopted the [Declaration on the Granting of Independence to Colonial Countries and Peoples](/wiki/Declaration_on_the_Granting_of_Independence_to_Colonial_Countries_and_Peoples "Declaration on the Granting of Independence to Colonial Countries and Peoples") in 1960 with no votes against but abstentions from all major colonial powers. The UN works towards decolonization through groups including the [UN Committee on Decolonization](/wiki/Special_Committee_on_Decolonization "Special Committee on Decolonization").[[207]](#cite_note-211) The committee lists seventeen remaining "[non-self-governing territories](/wiki/Non-self-governing_territories "Non-self-governing territories")", the largest and most populous of which is the [Western Sahara](/wiki/Western_Sahara "Western Sahara").[[208]](#cite_note-212)

The UN also declares and co-ordinates [international observances](/wiki/International_observance "International observance") that bring awareness to issues of international interest or concern; examples include [World Tuberculosis Day](/wiki/World_Tuberculosis_Day "World Tuberculosis Day"), [Earth Day](/wiki/Earth_Day "Earth Day"), and the [International Year of Deserts and Desertification](/wiki/International_Year_of_Deserts_and_Desertification "International Year of Deserts and Desertification").[[209]](#cite_note-213)

Starting in 2023, the [United Nations Office for Digital and Emerging Technologies](https://www.un.org/digital-emerging-technologies/) has organized an annual UN Open Source Week to facilitate collaborative and international technological projects, AI policies, and governance.[[210]](#cite_note-214)

The UN budget for 2026 was $3.45 billion, not including additional resources donated by members, such as peacekeeping forces.
Including [specialized agencies of the UN](/wiki/List_of_specialized_agencies_of_the_United_Nations "List of specialized agencies of the United Nations"), the UN System Chief Executives Board for Coordination reports total expenses of $67.4 billion in 2022 for 43 United Nations entities.[[212]](#cite_note-216)[[213]](#cite_note-217)

The UN is financed from assessed and voluntary contributions from its member states. The General Assembly approves the regular budget and determines the assessment for each member. This is broadly based on the relative capacity of each nation to pay, as measured by its [gross national income](/wiki/Gross_national_income "Gross national income") (or GNI), with adjustments for external debt and low per capita income.[[214]](#cite_note-fifth-2006-218)

The Assembly has established the principle that the UN should not be unduly dependent on any one member to finance its operations. Thus, there is a "ceiling" rate, setting the maximum amount that any member can be assessed for the regular budget. In December 2000, the Assembly revised the scale of assessments in response to pressure from the United States. As part of that revision, the regular budget ceiling was reduced from 25% to 22%.[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFasulo2004117-219) For the [least developed countries](/wiki/Least_developed_country "Least developed country") (or LDCs), a ceiling rate of 0.01% is applied.[[214]](#cite_note-fifth-2006-218) In addition to the ceiling rates, the minimum amount assessed to any member nation (or "floor" rate) is set at 0.001% of the UN budget ($31,000 for the two-year budget 2021–2022).[[216]](#cite_note-220)[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeissDaws2009682-221)

A large share of the UN's expenditure addresses its core mission of peace and security, and this budget is assessed separately from the main organizational budget.[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFasulo2004115-222) The peacekeeping budget for the 2025–2026 fiscal year is $5.38 billion, supporting 66,839 personnel deployed in 12 missions worldwide.[[219]](#cite_note-223) UN peace operations are funded by assessments, using a formula derived from the regular funding scale that includes a weighted surcharge for the five permanent Security Council members, who must approve all peacekeeping operations. This surcharge serves to offset discounted peacekeeping assessment rates for less developed countries. The largest contributors to the [UN peacekeeping](/wiki/Peacekeeping "Peacekeeping") budget for 2025–2026 are: the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") (26.158%), [China](/wiki/China "China") (23.785%), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") (6.930%), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") (5.694%), the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") (3.999%), [France](/wiki/France "France") (3.869%), [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") (2.816%), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") (2.543%), [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") (2.490%) and [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea") (2.349%).

Special UN programmes not included in the regular budget, such as [UNICEF](/wiki/UNICEF "UNICEF") and the World Food Programme, are financed by voluntary contributions from member governments, corporations, and private individuals.[[220]](#cite_note-224)[[221]](#cite_note-225)

## Assessments and reviews

Main articles: [Reform of the United Nations](/wiki/Reform_of_the_United_Nations "Reform of the United Nations") and [Reform of the United Nations Security Council](/wiki/Reform_of_the_United_Nations_Security_Council "Reform of the United Nations Security Council")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Friedensnobelpreis_2001_Vereinte_Nationen.jpg/250px-Friedensnobelpreis_2001_Vereinte_Nationen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Friedensnobelpreis_2001_Vereinte_Nationen.jpg)

The [2001 Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/2001_Nobel_Peace_Prize "2001 Nobel Peace Prize") to the UN—diploma in the lobby of the [UN Headquarters](/wiki/Headquarters_of_the_United_Nations "Headquarters of the United Nations") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City")

Several studies have examined the Security Council's responsiveness to armed conflict. Findings suggest that the council is more likely to meet and deliberate on conflicts that are more intense and have led to more humanitarian suffering, but that its responsiveness is also shaped by the political interests of member states and in particular of the permanent members.[[222]](#cite_note-226)

UN peacekeeping missions are assessed to be generally successful. A book looking at 47 peace operations by [Virginia Page Fortna](/wiki/Virginia_Page_Fortna "Virginia Page Fortna") of [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University") found that UN-led conflict resolution usually resulted in long-term peace.[[223]](#cite_note-227)

Political scientists Hanne Fjelde, Lisa Hultman and [Desiree Nilsson](/w/index.php?title=Desiree_Nilsson&action=edit&redlink=1 "Desiree Nilsson (page does not exist)") of [Uppsala University](/wiki/Uppsala_University "Uppsala University") studied twenty years of data on peacekeeping missions, concluding that they were more effective at reducing civilian casualties than counterterrorism operations by nation states.[[224]](#cite_note-228)

[Georgetown University](/wiki/Georgetown_University "Georgetown University") professor Lise Howard postulates that UN peacekeeping operations are more effective due to their emphasis on "verbal persuasion, financial inducements and coercion short of offensive military force, including surveillance and arrest", which are likelier to change the behavior of warring parties.[[159]](#cite_note-:0-163)

British historian [Paul Kennedy](/wiki/Paul_Kennedy "Paul Kennedy") states that while the organization has suffered some major setbacks, "when all its aspects are considered, the UN has brought great benefits to our generation and will bring benefits to our children's and grandchildren's generations as well."[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy2007290-229)

In 2012, then French President [François Hollande](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Hollande "François Hollande") stated that "France trusts the United Nations. She knows that no state, no matter how powerful, can solve urgent problems, fight for development and bring an end to all crises. France wants the UN to be the centre of global governance".[[226]](#cite_note-230) In his 1953 address to the United States Committee for United Nations Day, American President Dwight D. Eisenhower expressed his view that, for all its flaws, "the United Nations represents man's best organized hope to substitute the conference table for the battlefield".[[227]](#cite_note-231)

[Jacques Fomerand](/w/index.php?title=Jacques_Fomerand&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jacques Fomerand (page does not exist)"), a professor in political sciences, writes that the "accomplishments of the United Nations in the last 60 years are impressive in their own terms. Progress in human development during the 20th century has been dramatic, and the UN and its agencies have certainly helped the world become a more hospitable and livable place for millions".[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFomerand2009cviii-232)

Reviewing the first 50 years of the UN's history, the author [Stanley Meisler](/w/index.php?title=Stanley_Meisler&action=edit&redlink=1 "Stanley Meisler (page does not exist)") writes that "the United Nations never fulfilled the hopes of its founders, but it accomplished a great deal nevertheless", citing its role in decolonization and its many successful peacekeeping efforts.[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeisler1995339-233)

## Awards

A number of agencies and individuals associated with the UN have won the [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize") in recognition of their work. Two secretaries-general, Dag Hammarskjöld and Kofi Annan, were each awarded the prize; as were Ralph Bunche, a UN negotiator, René Cassin, a contributor to the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, and the American Secretary of State [Cordell Hull](/wiki/Cordell_Hull "Cordell Hull") for his role in the organization's founding. [Lester B. Pearson](/wiki/Lester_B._Pearson "Lester B. Pearson"), the Canadian [Secretary of State for External Affairs](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_External_Affairs "Secretary of State for External Affairs"), was awarded the prize in 1957 for his role in organizing the UN's first peacekeeping force to resolve the Suez Crisis.

UNICEF won the prize in 1965, the [International Labour Organization](/wiki/International_Labour_Organization "International Labour Organization") in 1969, the UN Peacekeeping Forces in 1988, the International Atomic Energy Agency (which reports to the UN) in 2005, and the UN-supported [Organisation for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons](/wiki/Organisation_for_the_Prohibition_of_Chemical_Weapons "Organisation for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons") in 2013. The UN High Commissioner for Refugees was awarded the prize in 1954 and 1981, becoming one of only two recipients to win the prize twice. The UN as a whole was awarded the prize in 2001, sharing it with Annan.[[230]](#cite_note-234) In 2007, the [IPCC](/wiki/Intergovernmental_Panel_on_Climate_Change "Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change") received the prize "for their efforts to build up and disseminate greater knowledge about man-made climate change, and to lay the foundations for the measures that are needed to counteract such change."[[231]](#cite_note-235)

On March 21, 2025, the joint Universities of [Leuven](/wiki/KU_Leuven "KU Leuven") and [Louvain](/wiki/UCLouvain "UCLouvain") (Belgium) awarded the UN an [honorary degree](/wiki/Honorary_degree "Honorary degree") which was given in the hands of [António Guterres](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_Guterres "António Guterres").

## Criticism

Main article: [Criticism of the United Nations](/wiki/Criticism_of_the_United_Nations "Criticism of the United Nations")

### Role

In a sometimes-misquoted statement, American President [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") stated in February 2003—referring to UN uncertainty towards Iraqi provocations under the Saddam Hussein regime—that "free nations will not allow the UN to fade into history as an ineffective, irrelevant debating society."[[232]](#cite_note-236)[[233]](#cite_note-237)[[234]](#cite_note-238)

In 2020, former American President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama"), in his memoir *[A Promised Land](/wiki/A_Promised_Land "A Promised Land")* noted, "In the middle of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"), the chances of reaching any consensus had been slim, which is why the UN had stood idle as [Soviet tanks rolled into Hungary](/wiki/Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956 "Hungarian Revolution of 1956") or [U.S. planes dropped napalm on the Vietnamese countryside](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"). Even after the Cold War, divisions within the Security Council continued to hamstring the UN's ability to tackle problems. Its member states lacked either the means or the collective will to reconstruct failing states like [Somalia](/wiki/Somalia "Somalia"), or prevent an ethnic slaughter in places like Sri Lanka."[[235]](#cite_note-239)[[236]](#cite_note-240)

Since its founding, there have been many calls for [reform of the UN](/wiki/Reform_of_the_United_Nations "Reform of the United Nations") but little consensus on how to do so. Some want the UN to play a greater or more effective role in world affairs, while others want its role reduced to humanitarian work.

### Representation and structure

Core features of the UN apparatus, such as the [veto](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_veto_power "United Nations Security Council veto power") privileges of some nations in the [Security Council](/wiki/Security_Council "Security Council"), are often described as fundamentally undemocratic, contrary to the UN mission, and a main cause of inaction on genocides and [crimes against humanity](/wiki/Crimes_against_humanity "Crimes against humanity").[[237]](#cite_note-241)[[238]](#cite_note-242)

Jacques Fomerand state that the most enduring divide in views of the UN is "the North–South split" between [richer Northern nations and developing Southern nations](/wiki/North%E2%80%93South_divide_in_the_World "North–South divide in the World"). Southern nations tend to favour a more empowered UN with a stronger General Assembly, allowing them a greater voice in world affairs, while Northern nations prefer an economically [laissez-faire](/wiki/Laissez-faire "Laissez-faire") UN that focuses on transnational threats such as terrorism.[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFomerand2009civ-243)

There have been numerous calls for the [UN Security Council's membership to be increased](/wiki/Reform_of_the_United_Nations_Security_Council "Reform of the United Nations Security Council"), for different ways of electing the UN's secretary-general, and for a [UN Parliamentary Assembly](/wiki/United_Nations_Parliamentary_Assembly "United Nations Parliamentary Assembly") (UNPA).[[240]](#cite_note-244)

### Exclusion of nations

See also: [United Nations list of non-self-governing territories](/wiki/United_Nations_list_of_non-self-governing_territories "United Nations list of non-self-governing territories") and [List of states with limited recognition](/wiki/List_of_states_with_limited_recognition "List of states with limited recognition")

After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the [French Committee of National Liberation](/wiki/French_Committee_of_National_Liberation "French Committee of National Liberation") was late to be recognized by the United States as the government of France, and so the country was initially excluded from the conferences that created the new organization. Future French president [Charles de Gaulle](/wiki/Charles_de_Gaulle "Charles de Gaulle") criticized the UN, famously calling it a *machin* (contraption), and was not convinced that a [global security](/wiki/Global_security "Global security") alliance would help maintain world peace, preferring [direct defence treaties](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_France "Foreign relations of France") between countries.[[241]](#cite_note-245)

Following the [Chinese Civil War](/wiki/Chinese_Civil_War "Chinese Civil War"), the government of China was disputed between the [Chinese Nationalist Party](/wiki/Kuomintang "Kuomintang") and the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party"). After the foundation of the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China") (PRC) on 1 October 1949, the government of the [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China "Republic of China") (ROC) retreated to the island of Taiwan,[[242]](#cite_note-246) continuing to claim that it was the sole government of China. After the civil war, the United Nations continued recognizing the ROC as the [official government of China](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly_Resolution_505_(VI) "United Nations General Assembly Resolution 505 (VI)"). In 1971, amid growing debate over the representation of the Chinese people on the mainland,[[243]](#cite_note-247) the General Assembly passed [a resolution](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly_Resolution_2758_(XXVI) "United Nations General Assembly Resolution 2758 (XXVI)") recognizing the PRC as *"the only legitimate representatives of China to the United Nations."*[[244]](#cite_note-248) Critics[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] allege that this position reflects a failure of the organization's development goals and guidelines,[[245]](#cite_note-249) and it garnered renewed scrutiny during the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), when Taiwan was denied membership into the World Health Organization despite its [relatively effective response to the virus](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Taiwan "COVID-19 pandemic in Taiwan").[[246]](#cite_note-250) Support for Taiwan's inclusion in the UN remains challenged by the People's Republic of China, which claims the territories controlled by Taiwan [as their own territory](/wiki/One_China "One China").[[247]](#cite_note-251)

### Independence

Throughout the Cold War, both the United States and the Soviet Union repeatedly accused the UN of favouring the other. In 1950, the Soviet Union boycotted the organization in protest to [China's seat at the UN Security Council](/wiki/China_and_the_United_Nations "China and the United Nations") being given to the anti-communist [Republic of China](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"). Three years later, the Soviets effectively forced the resignation of UN Secretary-General [Trygve Lie](/wiki/Trygve_Lie "Trygve Lie") by refusing to acknowledge his administration due to his support of the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War").[[248]](#cite_note-252)

Ironically, the United States had simultaneously scrutinized the UN for employing communists and Soviet sympathizers, following a high-profile accusation that [Alger Hiss](/wiki/Alger_Hiss "Alger Hiss"), an American who had taken part in the establishment of the UN, had been a Soviet spy. American Senator Joseph McCarthy claimed that the [UN Secretariat](/wiki/United_Nations_Secretariat "United Nations Secretariat") under Secretary-General Lie harboured American communists, leading to further pressure that the UN chief resign.[[249]](#cite_note-253) The United States saw nascent opposition to the UN in the 1960s, particularly amongst conservatives, with groups such as the [John Birch Society](/wiki/John_Birch_Society "John Birch Society") stating that the organization was an instrument for communism.[[250]](#cite_note-254) Popular opposition to the UN was expressed through bumper stickers and signs with slogans such as "Get the U.S. out of the U.N. and the U.N. out of the U.S.!" and "You can't spell communism without U.N."[[251]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeisler199572–73,_82-255)

### United States sovereignty

In the United States, there were concerns about supposed threats to national sovereignty, most notably promoted by the [John Birch Society](/wiki/John_Birch_Society "John Birch Society"), which mounted a nationwide campaign in opposition to the UN during the 1960s.[[252]](#cite_note-256)[[253]](#cite_note-257)[[254]](#cite_note-258)

Beginning in the 1990s, the same concern appeared with the [American Sovereignty Restoration Act](/wiki/American_Sovereignty_Restoration_Act "American Sovereignty Restoration Act"), which has been introduced multiple times in the [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress"). In 1997, an amendment containing the bill received a floor vote, with 54 representatives voting in favour.[[255]](#cite_note-259)[[256]](#cite_note-260) The 2007 version of the bill ([H.R. 1146](https://www.congress.gov/bill/110th-congress/house-bill/1146)) was authored by [U.S. Representative](/wiki/U.S._Representative "U.S. Representative") [Ron Paul](/wiki/Ron_Paul "Ron Paul"), to effect the United States' withdrawal from the United Nations. It would repeal various laws pertaining to the UN, terminate authorization for funds to be spent on the UN, terminate UN presence on American property, and withdraw diplomatic immunity for UN employees.[[257]](#cite_note-261) It would provide up to two years for the United States to withdraw.[[258]](#cite_note-lamb-262) The *[Yale Law Journal](/wiki/Yale_Law_Journal "Yale Law Journal")* cited the Act as proof that "the United States's complaints against the United Nations have intensified."[[259]](#cite_note-263) The most recent iteration, As of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=United_Nations&action=edit), is H.R.7806, introduced by [Mike D. Rogers](/wiki/Mike_Rogers_(Alabama_politician) "Mike Rogers (Alabama politician)").[[260]](#cite_note-264)

American [state lawmakers](/wiki/State_legislature_(United_States) "State legislature (United States)") have proposed legislation to block various UN programs deemed to threaten U.S. sovereignty.[[261]](#cite_note-265) In 2023, [Tennessee](/wiki/Tennessee "Tennessee") enacted legislation to block the implementation of programs "originating in, or traceable to, the United Nations or a subsidiary entity of the United Nations," including [Agenda 21](/wiki/Agenda_21 "Agenda 21") and [the 2030 Agenda](/wiki/Sustainable_Development_Goals "Sustainable Development Goals").[[262]](#cite_note-266)[[263]](#cite_note-267)

### Alleged pro-Palestinian bias

The UN's attention to Israel's treatment of Palestinians has been considered excessive by Israeli diplomat [Dore Gold](/wiki/Dore_Gold "Dore Gold") and pro-Israeli individuals and organisations such as British scholar [Robert S. Wistrich](/wiki/Robert_S._Wistrich "Robert S. Wistrich"), American legal scholar [Alan Dershowitz](/wiki/Alan_Dershowitz "Alan Dershowitz"), Australian politician [Mark Dreyfus](/wiki/Mark_Dreyfus "Mark Dreyfus"), and the [Anti-Defamation League](/wiki/Anti-Defamation_League "Anti-Defamation League").[[264]](#cite_note-268) The UNHRC has likewise been accused of anti-Israel bias by Ex-President of the United States [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush"), who complained that the Council focused too much attention on Israel and not enough on adversaries of the US such as Cuba, Venezuela, North Korea and Iran.[[265]](#cite_note-269) In her confirmation hearing before the Senate panel to be the U.S. ambassador to the United Nations, [Elise Stefanik](/wiki/Elise_Stefanik "Elise Stefanik"), described the UN's attitude toward Israel as "anti-semitic".[[266]](#cite_note-d827-270)[[267]](#cite_note-271)[[268]](#cite_note-272)[[269]](#cite_note-273)

### Effectiveness

According to [international relations](/wiki/International_relations "International relations") scholar [Edward Luck](/wiki/Edward_Luck "Edward Luck"), the United States has preferred a feeble United Nations in major projects undertaken by the organization to forestall UN interference with, or resistance to, American policies. "The last thing the U.S. wants is an independent UN throwing its weight around", Luck said. Similarly, former [US Ambassador to the United Nations](/wiki/US_Ambassador_to_the_United_Nations "US Ambassador to the United Nations") [Daniel Patrick Moynihan](/wiki/Daniel_Patrick_Moynihan "Daniel Patrick Moynihan") explained that "The Department of State desired that the United Nations prove utterly ineffective in whatever measures it undertook. The task was given to me, and I carried it forward with not inconsiderable success."[[270]](#cite_note-274)

In 1994, former special representative of the secretary-general of the UN to Somalia [Mohamed Sahnoun](/wiki/Mohamed_Sahnoun "Mohamed Sahnoun") published *Somalia: The Missed Opportunities*,[[271]](#cite_note-275) a book in which he analyses the reasons for the failure of the 1992 UN intervention in [Somalia](/wiki/Somalia "Somalia"). Sahnoun claims that between the start of the Somali civil war in 1988 and the fall of the [Siad Barre](/wiki/Siad_Barre "Siad Barre") regime in January 1991, the UN missed at least three opportunities to prevent major human tragedies. When the UN tried to provide humanitarian assistance, they were totally outperformed by [NGOs](/wiki/NGO "NGO"), whose competence and dedication sharply contrasted with the UN's excessive caution and bureaucratic inefficiencies. Sahnoun warned that if radical reform were not undertaken, then the UN would continue to respond to such crises with inept improvisation.[[272]](#cite_note-276)

Beyond specific instances or areas of alleged ineffectiveness, some scholars debate the overall effectiveness of the UN. Adherents to the [realist](/wiki/Realism_(international_relations) "Realism (international relations)") school of international relations take a pessimistic position, arguing that the UN is not an effective organization because it is dominated and constrained by great powers. [Liberal](/wiki/Liberalism_(international_relations) "Liberalism (international relations)") scholars counter that it is an effective organization because it has proved capable of solving many problems by working around the restrictions imposed by powerful member states. The UN is generally considered by scholars to be more effective in realms such as public health, and humanitarian assistance.[[273]](#cite_note-277) The ineffectiveness of enforcing [territorial integrity](/wiki/Territorial_integrity "Territorial integrity") in the 21st century[[274]](#cite_note-278) have led to debate on possible re-emergence of the [right of conquest](/wiki/Right_of_conquest "Right of conquest").[[275]](#cite_note-279)

### Inefficiency and corruption

Critics have also accused the UN of bureaucratic inefficiency, waste, and corruption. In 1976, the General Assembly established the [Joint Inspection Unit](/wiki/Joint_Inspection_Unit "Joint Inspection Unit") to seek out inefficiencies within the UN system. During the 1990s, the United States withheld dues citing inefficiency and only started repayment on the condition that a major reforms initiative be introduced. In 1994, the [Office of Internal Oversight Services](/wiki/United_Nations_Office_of_Internal_Oversight_Services "United Nations Office of Internal Oversight Services") (or the OIOS) was established by the General Assembly to serve as an efficiency watchdog.[[276]](#cite_note-280)

In 2004, the UN faced accusations that its recently ended [Oil-for-Food Programme](/wiki/Oil-for-Food_Programme "Oil-for-Food Programme") — in which Iraq had been allowed to trade oil for basic needs to relieve the pressure of sanctions — had suffered from widespread corruption, including billions of dollars of [kickbacks](/wiki/Kickback_(bribery) "Kickback (bribery)"). An independent inquiry created by the UN found that many of its officials had been involved in [the scheme](/wiki/Oil-for-Food_Program_Hearings "Oil-for-Food Program Hearings"), and raised significant questions about the role of [Kojo Annan](/wiki/Kojo_Annan "Kojo Annan"), the son of [Kofi Annan](/wiki/Kofi_Annan "Kofi Annan").[[277]](#cite_note-281)

## Hymn to the United Nations

Main article: [Hymn to the United Nations](/wiki/Hymn_to_the_United_Nations "Hymn to the United Nations")

On the request of then United Nations [Secretary-General](/wiki/Secretary-General_of_the_United_Nations "Secretary-General of the United Nations") [U Thant](/wiki/U_Thant "U Thant"), a Hymn to the United Nations was performed on the occasion of its 26th anniversary, on 24 October 1971, by [Pau Casals](/wiki/Pau_Casals "Pau Casals"), the lyrics to which were penned by the poet [W. H. Auden](/wiki/W._H._Auden "W. H. Auden").[[278]](#cite_note-282)

Thant first approached Casals, who was a personal friend, looking to create a hymn to peace and hoping for the song to be based on the preamble of the [Charter of the United Nations](/wiki/Charter_of_the_United_Nations "Charter of the United Nations"). Thant later commissioned Auden to write the poem after Casals requested one to set to music. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]Auden completed his work in three days time. The finished work, scored for chorus and orchestra, takes approximately seven minutes to play. However, there were never any plans to adopt the song as the organization's official anthem.

For details, see [Holy See and the United Nations](/wiki/Holy_See_and_the_United_Nations "Holy See and the United Nations").
2. **[^](#cite_ref-39)** Poland had not been represented among the fifty nations at the San Francisco conference due to the reluctance of the Western superpowers to recognize its post-war communist government. 3. **[^](#cite_ref-140)** Some sources identify seventeen specialized agencies, taking into account the three specialized agencies that make up the [World Bank Group](/wiki/World_Bank_Group "World Bank Group"), which is now treated as one organization: the [International Bank for Reconstruction and Development](/wiki/International_Bank_for_Reconstruction_and_Development "International Bank for Reconstruction and Development") (IBRD), the [International Development Association](/wiki/International_Development_Association "International Development Association") (IDA), and the [International Finance Corporation](/wiki/International_Finance_Corporation "International Finance Corporation") (IFC).
4. **[^](#cite_ref-145)** The Holy See is a [sovereign entity](/wiki/Sovereignty "Sovereignty") with [statehood](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)") over the territory of the [Vatican City State](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City").
